decks.name: Sounds & Alphabet
decks.description: Every letter of the Azerbaijani alphabet with an example word. Learn to read any word and pronounce it roughly right. ~1 week.

decks.name: Dad & Family
decks.description: Pronouns, 'I am / you are', family words, greetings and the first heart phrases. ~2 weeks.

decks.name: Listening — First Words
decks.description: Hear Azerbaijani, recall the meaning. The goal is spoken understanding.

decks.name: Phrases for Dad
decks.description: Your own phrases to say to Dad. Add one any time it occurs to you.

decks.name: Everyday Survival
decks.description: Numbers, 'I have / want / like', food and home, present tense, question words. ~3–4 weeks.

decks.name: Patterns
decks.description: Fill-in-the-blank patterns — swap the word to build your own sentences.

decks.name: Call with Dad
decks.description: Short scripted conversations you can run end-to-end, then say for real.

decks.name: Everyday Phrases
decks.description: High-frequency phrases for real conversations — learn by whole phrases.

decks.name: Dad's Roots
decks.description: Ask Dad about his childhood in Sumgait and the world he grew up in.

cards.hook: the #1 reading trap: c = 'j' as in 'jam' [дж]
schwa — the most common vowel; like the 'a' in 'cat'
soft, near-silent; it lengthens the vowel before it. Never starts a word.
dotless ı — back unrounded vowel [Russian ы]
q ≈ a hard 'g' made further back
rounded front vowel, like German ö
rounded front vowel, like German ü

cards.note: Use with family and friends — perfect for Dad.
No gender in Azerbaijani — 'o' covers he, she and it.
The -un ending is the polite/plural form.
yaxşı (good) + -yam (I am).
From sevmək (to love).
From istəmək (to want).
From bilmək (to know).
səni (you, object) + sevirəm (I love).
mənim (my) + ata + -m (my) + -san (you are).
Everyday way to say it. More literary: 'Səndən ötrü darıxmışam'.
Warm word for father; in some regions it means grandfather.
Natural reply to 'Necəsən?'
Turn the question back to Dad.
Reply to a good wish, e.g. 'Sabahın xeyir' → 'Sənə də'.

cards.en: I want to talk with you.

cards.note: Azerbaijani uses 'var' (exists) for having: 'Pulum var' = I have money.
Opposite of 'var': 'Vaxtım yoxdu' = I don't have time.
From getmək (to go).
From gəlmək (to come).
From görmək (to see).
From etmək (to do).
Casual form of 'düzdür'.
Swap the noun: Çay istəyirəm · Çörək istəyirəm.
Add -ım/-im to the noun: Vaxtım var · Sualım var.
The opposite of var: Pulum yoxdu.
Try: Səni görürəm (I see you).
Dative -ə/-a marks the destination.
Same word for 'food' and 'to eat'.
Said to someone who is eating or about to eat.
bu gün (today) + hansı (which) + gün (day).
baş (head) + -ım (my) + ağrıyır (hurts).

cards.exEn: In the morning I drink tea.

cards.note: Said at bedtime. Short form: 'Gecən xeyrə'.
To one person (informal). Reply: 'Xoş gördük'.
Warm way to end a call. Literally 'stay in health'.
Warmer, fuller way to say it than just 'Darıxmışam'. sənin üçün = for you.
fəxr etmək = to be proud. A strong thing to tell your father.

cards.en: You are very dear to me.
cards.note: əziz = dear/precious.
həmişə = always; ürəyimdəsən = you are in my heart.

cards.en: It's so nice to hear your voice.
cards.note: Lovely to say at the start of a phone call. səs = voice.
A warm way to end a call. Literally: look after yourself well.
tez = soon; görüşmək = to meet/see each other.
A caring question to ask Dad. səhhət = health.
etdin = you did (past). A natural everyday question.
Easy small-talk question. hava = weather; orada = there.
Very useful when you miss something Dad said.
bir də = once more; de = say (command).
yavaş = slowly. Helps Dad slow down for you.
Swap the noun: İşin necədir? (How's work?) · Anan necədir? (How's your mum?)
Past tense -din ending = you did. Try: Harada oldun? (Where were you?)
Swap the verb: Səninlə görüşmək istəyirəm (I want to meet with you).
Swap: Evimiz üçün darıxmışam (I miss our home).
də = too/also. Reply to Dad: Mən də yaxşıyam (I'm good too).
Say it warmly; a rising tone turns it into a gentle question.
Literally 'how did your childhood pass?'
'danış' is the informal 'tell', right for your dad.

cards.en: Where did you live when you were little?
cards.note: 'yaşayırdın' = used to live (past continuous).
'o vaxtlar' = those times / back then.

cards.en: What did your father do for work?
cards.note: The natural way to ask about someone's job.
The '-mi' on 'idi' makes a clear yes/no question.

cards.en: What games did you play as a child?

cards.note: 'çox' (very) makes it warm and encouraging.
'məhəllə' carries a warm, communal feeling.

cards.en: What were you afraid of as a kid?
cards.note: '-kən' = 'when (you were)'.

cards.en: Which subject did you like in school?
How did you celebrate the holidays?
What did you do in summer?
Do you remember the house you were born in?
